findings.message: "VITE_SECRET_KEY" exposes a secret to the browser. The VITE_ prefix makes this variable public in your build output.
findings.fix: Remove the "VITE_" prefix from this variable. Server-side secrets must NOT use client-prefixed env vars. Access it only in server-side code (API routes, server components, middleware).

findings.message: Storage rules allow unrestricted access. Anyone on the internet can read/write your data.
findings.evidence: "evidence": "prompt: `SECURITY FIX: My Firebase/Firestore security rules allow public read/write access. Replace \"allow read, write: if true\" with proper rules. At minimum: \"allow read, write: if request.auth != null\" (requires login). Better: add per-collection rules that check request.auth.uid matches the document's userId field. Test rules in the Firebase Emulator before deploying. Never use \"if true\" in production.`,",
findings.fix: Require authentication: "allow read, write: if request.auth != null;" at minimum. Better: add granular per-collection rules that validate request.auth.uid matches the document owner.

findings.message: Rule allows unrestricted access (if true). Anyone on the internet can read/write your data.
findings.fix: Require authentication: "allow read, write: if request.auth != null;" at minimum. Better: add granular per-collection rules that validate request.auth.uid matches the document owner.

findings.message: eval() with dynamic input — code injection risk
findings.fix: Sanitize all user input before use. For HTML: use a sanitization library or textContent instead of innerHTML. For SQL: use parameterized queries. For shell: use allowlists, never interpolate user input into commands.

findings.message: SQL query built with string interpolation — SQL injection risk
findings.evidence: "evidence": "const query = db.execute(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '${userInput}'`); // SQL injection",
findings.fix: Sanitize all user input before use. For HTML: use a sanitization library or textContent instead of innerHTML. For SQL: use parameterized queries. For shell: use allowlists, never interpolate user input into commands.

findings.fix: Never expose the service_role key to the client. Use the anon key for browser Supabase calls, and keep service_role usage in server-only code (route handlers, server actions, or files importing "server-only").

findings.message: Storage rules allow unrestricted access. Anyone on the internet can read/write your data.
findings.evidence: "evidence": "\"evidence\": \"prompt: `SECURITY FIX: My Firebase/Firestore security rules allow public read/write access. Replace \\\"allow read, write: if true\\\" with proper rules. At minimum: \\\"allow read, write: if request.auth != null\\\" (requires login). Better: add per-collection rules that check request.auth.uid matches the document's userId field. Test rules in the Firebase Emulator before deploying. Never use \\\"if true\\\" in production.`,\",",
findings.fix: Require authentication: "allow read, write: if request.auth != null;" at minimum. Better: add granular per-collection rules that validate request.auth.uid matches the document owner.

findings.message: eval() with dynamic input — code injection risk
findings.fix: Sanitize all user input before use. For HTML: use a sanitization library or textContent instead of innerHTML. For SQL: use parameterized queries. For shell: use allowlists, never interpolate user input into commands.

findings.message: Stripe webhook handler does not verify the signature — anyone can send fake events.
findings.fix: Verify the webhook: const event = stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(rawBody, sig, process.env.STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET). Never parse the body as JSON first — use the raw body.

findings.message: Storage rules allow unrestricted access. Anyone on the internet can read/write your data.
findings.evidence: prompt: `SECURITY FIX: My Firebase/Firestore security rules allow public read/write access. Replace "allow read, write: if true" with proper rules. At minimum: "allow read, write: if request.auth != null" (requires login). Better: add per-collection rules that check request.auth.uid matches the document's userId field. Test rules in the Firebase Emulator before deploying. Never use "if true" in production.`,
findings.fix: Require authentication: "allow read, write: if request.auth != null;" at minimum. Better: add granular per-collection rules that validate request.auth.uid matches the document owner.

findings.message: TLS certificate verification disabled
findings.evidence: prompt: `SECURITY FIX: I have insecure connection configurations. Find and fix: (1) HTTP URLs pointing to production ser
findings.fix: Never disable TLS verification. Fix the underlying certificate issue instead. In production this allows man-in-the-middle attacks.

findings.message: Function "GET" uses a user-supplied ID without verifying ownership in the same function scope.
findings.fix: After extracting the ID, verify ownership within the same function: "if (resource.userId !== session.user.id) return 403".

findings.message: Function "uploadHandler" handles file uploads without type/size validation in scope.
findings.fix: Validate MIME type against an allowlist and set a max file size. The HTML accept="" attribute is NOT security.

findings.message: Function "POST" passes raw request body to a database operation without destructuring or schema validation first.
findings.evidence: const user = await prisma.user.create({ data: body });
findings.fix: Destructure only expected fields: "const { name, email } = req.body" then pass those explicitly. Or validate with Zod: "schema.parse(req.body)".

findings.message: Find-then-create race condition (possible duplicate) — concurrent requests can cause inconsistency.
findings.fix: Use database transactions or atomic operations. For Prisma: prisma.$transaction(). For MongoDB: $inc for counters. For SQL: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE within a transaction.

findings.message: Webhook handler does not verify the request signature. Anyone who knows the URL can send fake events (e.g., fake "payment succeeded").
findings.fix: Always verify webhook signatures before processing events. For Stripe: use stripe.webhooks.constructEvent(body, sig, secret). For other providers: verify the HMAC signature using your webhook secret. Without verification, attackers can POST fake events to trigger actions like granting access, issuing refunds, or creating accounts.

findings.message: Sensitive data stored in browser storage — visible in DevTools → Application tab
findings.fix: Don't store tokens or sensitive data in localStorage/sessionStorage. Use httpOnly cookies (set by the server) for auth tokens — they're invisible to JavaScript and DevTools Application tab. For session data, keep it server-side.

findings.message: Direct innerHTML assignment with dynamic, unescaped value — potential XSS vector
findings.fix: Escape/sanitize the value before assigning to innerHTML (e.g. an esc()/DOMPurify wrapper), or use textContent for plain text.

findings.message: eval() with dynamic input — code injection risk
findings.fix: Sanitize all user input before use. For HTML: use a sanitization library or textContent instead of innerHTML. For SQL: use parameterized queries. For shell: use allowlists, never interpolate user input into commands.

findings.message: SQL query built with string interpolation — SQL injection risk
findings.evidence: const query = db.execute(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '${userInput}'`); // SQL injection

findings.message: Debug mode enabled — may expose internal state in production

findings.fix: Use environment variables for debug flags: "debug: process.env.NODE_ENV === 'development'". Never hardcode debug:true. Disable GraphQL introspection/playground in production. Debug mode exposes stack traces, internal routes, database queries, and environment variables to anyone.

findings.message: Password reset token generated with Math.random() — predictable.
findings.evidence: prompt: `SECURITY FIX: I'm using Math.random() for security-sensitive values like tokens, session IDs, or reset codes. M
findings.fix: Use crypto.randomBytes(32).toString("hex") for reset tokens. Never use Math.random() for security tokens.

findings.message: MFA verification endpoint may not be linked to the login session — could be called independently.
findings.fix: Link MFA verification to the login session. Issue a temporary "pending MFA" token after password auth, require it for the MFA verify endpoint, and only issue a full session after both steps pass.

findings.message: JWT issued without expiration — token is valid forever if compromised.
findings.evidence: prompt: `SECURITY FIX: My JWT implementation has security issues. Check my JWT code for: (1) Weak signing secret — repla
findings.fix: Add expiry to jwt.sign: jwt.sign(payload, secret, { expiresIn: "1h" }). Use short-lived access tokens (15min-1h) with refresh tokens for longer sessions.

findings.message: API route calls a paid service but has no rate limiting. A bot or abusive user could run up your bill.
findings.fix: Add rate limiting before calling paid APIs or exposing sensitive actions. Use Upstash Ratelimit (serverless-friendly) or express-rate-limit. Set sensible per-user and global limits.

findings.message: Next.js mutation handler — verify CSRF protection is in place
findings.fix: Add CSRF protection to state-changing endpoints. Options: (1) Use SameSite=Lax or Strict cookies. (2) Implement double-submit cookie pattern. (3) Check Origin/Referer headers. (4) Use a CSRF token library like csrf-csrf. Without CSRF protection, attackers can trigger actions on behalf of your users from external sites.

findings.message: AI API call has no max_tokens limit — uncapped cost per request.

findings.message: Config file is missing security headers: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy
findings.evidence: Missing: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy
findings.fix: Add security headers to your deployment config or middleware. At minimum set: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Strict-Transport-Security, and Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin.

findings.message: Production source maps enabled in Next.js — full source code exposed
findings.fix: Disable source maps for production builds, or use "hidden-source-map" which generates maps but doesn't reference them in the bundle. Source maps let anyone see your full original code, comments, and variable names in DevTools → Sources.

findings.message: Auth check in useEffect — runs client-side only, server data is still exposed
findings.fix: Client-side auth only hides UI elements — it does NOT protect data. Always verify auth server-side in your API routes/middleware too. Anyone can open DevTools Console and call fetch("/your/api/endpoint") directly.

findings.message: Price/total calculated client-side with arithmetic — can be modified via DevTools Console before submission
findings.evidence: const totalPrice = cart.reduce((sum, item) => sum + item.price * item.quantity, 0);
findings.fix: Never trust client-side values for pricing, permissions, or business logic. Always recalculate/revalidate on the server. The client is the user's machine — they control everything on it. DevTools lets anyone modify any value, re-enable any button, and edit any request before it's sent.

findings.message: SQL SELECT without LIMIT clause — returns all matching rows
findings.evidence: const rawTerms = await sql('SELECT id, term, definition, eli5_definition as "eli5Definition", category, example FROM ter
findings.fix: Always add pagination or limits to list endpoints. Use limit/offset, cursor-based pagination, or at minimum a hard cap (e.g., .limit(100)). Without limits: (1) anyone can scrape your entire database in one request, (2) large result sets crash your server or client, (3) it's a trivial DoS vector.

findings.message: Sensitive data in console.log — visible in DevTools Console
findings.evidence: console.log('[x-sync] Skipping scheduled sync: missing credentials')
findings.fix: Remove console.log statements that output sensitive data before deploying. Use a proper logging library with log levels and strip debug/info logs in production builds. Anyone can open DevTools → Console and see everything you logged.

findings.message: Config file is missing security headers: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy
findings.evidence: Missing: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Strict-Transport-Security, Referrer-Policy
findings.fix: Add security headers to your deployment config or middleware. At minimum set: Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, Strict-Transport-Security, and Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin.

findings.message: Headers are configured but X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors is missing — vulnerable to clickjacking.
findings.fix: Add X-Frame-Options: DENY header, or use CSP frame-ancestors: 'none'. If using helmet: helmet() sets this by default.
